prompts.prompt: We're migrating to an event-driven architecture. Our architect says this will make our services loosely coupled. How should I evaluate that claim?
prompts.why: Direct invocation: evaluating an EDA loose-coupling claim requires the coupling diagnosis framework

prompts.prompt: We publish an OrderPlaced event on SNS and fan out to 14 downstream services. We renamed a field last week and had to update 11 of those services. Why is our 'loosely coupled' event bus causing so much coordination?
prompts.why: Classic growth trap: topology-decoupled but data-format coupled; the diagnosis framework explains why

prompts.prompt: We use AWS EventBridge as our event bus. When I add a new consumer, I have to add a rule target. Is this real Pub-Sub or something else?
prompts.why: Direct Recipient List trap question about EventBridge — the core asymmetric coupling diagnosis

prompts.prompt: Our team is debating whether to name our messages as events (OrderPlaced, UserRegistered) versus commands (ProcessOrder, CreateUser). Does the naming choice affect how decoupled the system is?
prompts.why: Event semantics vs. channel topology confusion — the framework clarifies that naming changes nothing about coupling dimensions

prompts.prompt: We receive events from an AWS service (S3 bucket notifications). Should we use Pub-Sub or point-to-point for our internal consumers? We don't control the S3 event format.
prompts.why: Uncontrolled source scenario — the framework's key decision: use Pub-Sub when you don't control the source

prompts.prompt: How does switching from REST APIs to an event bus change the coupling between our microservices? We want to understand concretely what gets better and what stays the same.
prompts.why: RPC-to-async-messaging comparison — requires running the coupling comparison table across dimensions

prompts.prompt: We have 40 microservices all subscribing to a shared CustomerUpdated event. We need to add a new field to it. What are we dealing with here?
prompts.why: Growth trap diagnosis: 40 subscribers are data-format coupled despite topology decoupling; requires the framework

prompts.prompt: A vendor is pitching us their event mesh solution, claiming it will decouple all our services. What questions should I ask to evaluate that claim?
prompts.why: Evaluating a vendor loose-coupling claim requires the dimensional diagnosis framework

prompts.prompt: How do I configure dead letter queues on AWS SQS for messages that fail processing?
prompts.why: Dead Letter Channel configuration question — operational, not a coupling diagnosis task

prompts.prompt: What is the difference between a topic and a queue in Apache Kafka?
prompts.why: Kafka terminology question — basic concepts, not an EDA coupling diagnosis

prompts.prompt: We need to process 10,000 messages per second from SQS. How many consumers should we run?
prompts.why: Throughput and scaling question — Competing Consumers decision, not coupling diagnosis

prompts.prompt: Should I use REST or gRPC for communication between two services in the same Kubernetes cluster?
prompts.why: RPC technology selection question — integration style choice but not an EDA coupling analysis

prompts.prompt: How do I implement idempotency in my message consumer to handle duplicate deliveries?
prompts.why: Idempotent Receiver implementation question — not an EDA coupling diagnosis

prompts.prompt: We have microservices A and B. A publishes an event; B subscribes. Someone says we're 'decoupled.' What does that actually mean and is it true?
prompts.why: Blurred: could be a basic Pub-Sub explanation OR the full coupling diagnosis (which coupling dimensions, is it true Pub-Sub, do A and B share a schema). The diagnosis framework gives a richer answer than a simple explanation.

prompts.prompt: We're choosing between calling our downstream services synchronously via REST versus publishing an event and letting them subscribe. Which approach gives us better decoupling?
prompts.why: Blurred: this is an integration style selection question (f01) AND an EDA coupling diagnosis question (f03). The coupling comparison table is the right tool but integration style selection is the framing.
